 {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4r2ib","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:09:03.405716Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.545322Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.544924Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 0761bb33: mission dispatch now calls WorkflowRunner::handle_detection and returns real start metadata","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,
  - every [[safety.rules]] custom gate,
  - the [safety.command_gate] allow/deny list.

Site 2 (main.rs:20511, RobotCommands::Workflow::Abort): identical construction. abort_execution_with_cx runs compensation steps through the same injector; any send_text in a compensation path bypasses the same three surfaces.

Compare the correctly-gated paths:
  - RobotCommands::Send at main.rs:17652 uses config.safety.require_prompt_active AND .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules.
  - Non-robot workflow invocations at main.rs:21361, 21533, 25051, 27347 all wire the full policy surface.

NOT a duplicate of ft-48ah6 (read-only policy): this bead is specifically about the write/mutation surface (workflow's send_text / command-bearing steps). ft-48ah6 covered the read/search side.

Fix: replace the hardcoded 'false' with config.safety.require_prompt_active at both sites, and chain .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()) before passing the engine to PolicyGatedInjector. Rate-limit args are already correct.

Regression test: a send inside a robot-invoked workflow must be DENIED when config.safety.require_prompt_active=true AND no prompt is active, matching the direct-send gate behavior. Currently it is allowed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:46:59.925741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084786Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084480Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 834b59f2. Both RobotCommands::Workflow::Run (main.rs:19831) and ::Abort (main.rs:20520) now build PolicyEngine with config.safety.require_prompt_active and chain .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules — matching the construction at RobotCommands::Send (main.rs:17652) and non-robot workflow sites (21361/21533/25051/27347). Regression test robot_workflow_policy_engine_denies_send_when_prompt_required_ft_4z7vh asserts SendText is denied when require_prompt_active=true + command_running. Test binary compile is currently blocked by pre-existing concurrent-agent breakage (E0603 macro select private, E0063 missing defer_fts_triggers field) unrelated to this fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-6269x","title":"[reality-gap] ft snapshot restore Unix-only status is not disclosed in README session-persistence matrix","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:128 Session Persistence matrix row:

    | Session persistence | Supported with backend prerequisite |
    Snapshots, session inspection, restore, and `ft session doctor`
    are implemented, but live restore still depends on the current
    WezTerm mux interop boundary. \`ft restart\` is currently
    Unix-only. |

Disclosed: `ft restart` Unix-only.
Undisclosed: `ft snapshot restore` has the *same* cfg gate.

# Reality (code)

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:27100` (ft restart — disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft restart is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:39715` (ft snapshot restore — NOT disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft snapshot restore is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

Same gate, same failure mode. README line 128 specifically mentions
`ft restart` as the Unix-only surface but omits `ft snapshot restore`,
which is also mentioned in the *same* matrix row ("Snapshots, session
inspection, **restore**, and `ft session doctor` are implemented").

A Windows operator reading the matrix is told "restore is
implemented" (true, on Unix) without the cross-platform caveat.

# Failure shape

Operator on Windows runs `ft snapshot restore ...` → eprintln hits
→ exit 1. Workaround unknown.

# Recommended disposition

Update README.md:128 to say:

    Snapshots and session inspection are implemented cross-platform;
    `ft restart` and `ft snapshot restore` are currently Unix-only.

One-line doc change. Alternative: fix the cfg gate. The session
persistence code under `#[cfg(unix)]` likely depends on real Unix
signals / fork semantics — porting is non-trivial. Docs-first is
the pragmatic path.

# Related

ft-2guux previously flagged G2 "`ft restart` Unix-only not in
support matrix" and was closed 2026-04-21 with a README.md:128
update. That update added disclosure for `ft restart` but missed
`ft snapshot restore` — this bead is the follow-up.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"pane8","owner":"pane3-readme-fix","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:55.113196Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:55.619147Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:49:57.208098Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","platform","reality-gap"]}
 {"id":"ft-682n6","title":"Migrate ~50 tokio-based async tests to asupersync runtime (ft-22x4r tracked)","description":"# Goal
Convert ~50 tests using #[tokio::test] to work with the asupersync runtime. These tests fail at runtime with: task::spawn called outside of Runtime::block_on context. Originally tracked as ft-22x4r.

# Background
The asupersync migration replaced tokio, but ~50 tests still use #[tokio::test]. Count verified 2026-04-06: 50 in .rs files (excluding .bak). Most are in *_labruntime.rs test files (2 per file across ~25 files) plus 2 in dependency_eradication.rs.

# Design
## Discovery (exact count at implementation time)
1. grep -r '#\[tokio::test\]' crates/ --include='*.rs' | grep -v '.bak' > /tmp/tokio_tests.txt
2. Count and categorize by file pattern:
   - *_labruntime.rs files: ~25 files × 2 tests = ~50 tests (async smoke + basic scenario)
   - dependency_eradication.rs: 2 tests
3. For each, identify tokio primitives used: spawn, sleep, channels, timeout

## Migration strategy
- *_labruntime.rs pattern: These all follow the same template (2 tests per file). Once one is migrated, the rest are mechanical.
  Replace: #[tokio::test] with #[test] + runtime_compat::block_on(async { ... })
  Or: if asupersync provides a test macro, use that
- Replace tokio::spawn -> runtime_compat spawn equivalent
- Replace tokio::time::sleep -> runtime_compat::sleep
- Replace tokio channels -> runtime_compat channels

## Batch approach
Since *_labruntime.rs files follow a uniform pattern, write a migration helper that does the mechanical replacement, then verify each file compiles.

## Structured Logging
- Migration log: file, old_macro, new_macro, primitives_replaced, compile_status

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Zero #[tokio::test] in .rs files (verified by grep, excluding .bak).
2. All ~50 previously-failing tests now pass.
3. No semantic changes — same assertions.
4. Total test count preserved.
5. No new tokio dependencies.
6. Commit message lists affected files.

# Implementation Notes
- runtime_compat.rs provides bridge primitives.
- The *_labruntime.rs naming convention suggests these are lab/smoke tests for async behavior.
- This bead unblocks ft-xbnl0.2 (async cutover epic).
- Coordinate with ft-n2j5w (MCP test fix) if any MCP tests also use tokio::test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:21.227738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.155157Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.154811Z","close_reason":"Verified: zero actual #[tokio::test] annotations remain in crates/frankenterm-core/. All 50 occurrences found by grep are in comments/docstrings/string literals describing the migration that was already done. The *_labruntime.rs files already use #[test] + RuntimeFixture::current_thread().block_on(). No code changes needed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","migration","testing"]}

 {"id":"ft-6gqga","title":"[stub] workflows/wait_execution.rs:199-204: WaitCondition::External permanently returns Unsupported","description":"# Genuine production stub

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/wait_execution.rs:199-204` — `WaitConditionExecutor::execute_wait` arm for `WaitCondition::External { key }`.

```rust
WaitCondition::External { key } => {
    // External signals are not implemented in this layer
    Ok(WaitConditionResult::Unsupported {
        reason: format!("External signal '{key}' requires external signal registry"),
    })
}
```

This is not a platform stub (the pattern-wait and timeout-wait arms above it are fully implemented) — `External` is a first-class variant of the public `WaitCondition` enum but the execution path has NEVER been wired. Every caller that constructs `WaitCondition::External { key }` silently gets `Unsupported` back. The error hint names 'external signal registry' as a missing dependency, but no such registry exists anywhere in the crate (`rg 'external.*signal.*registry' crates/frankenterm-core/src` returns zero hits).

# Scope — NOT the same as the frankensqlite family

Recorder/ingest/reindex FrankenSqlite stubs are tracked under the BLOCKED wa-2l27x.8 FrankenSQLite migration plan and were narratively disclosed via ft-trby3. The `WaitCondition::External` stub has no parent bead — it's an orphaned production gap.

# Fix options

1. **Remove the variant.** If no caller actually constructs `External`, the enum should not expose the variant. Git blame shows the variant was added speculatively; `rg 'WaitCondition::External' crates` finds only the match arm and the enum declaration — no producer. Cleanest fix: delete the variant + match arm, file the follow-up when an external-signal feature is actually designed.

2. **Implement the registry.** Introduce a `SignalRegistry` trait with pub fn `signal(key: &str)` / `wait(key: &str, timeout: Duration) -> future`. Thread it through `WaitConditionExecutor::new`. Non-trivial — requires designing signal lifetime/TTL semantics.

Recommend option (1) pending a real consumer. Either way, `WaitConditionResult::Unsupported` has no legitimate emit site after the fix.

# Acceptance

Either `WaitCondition::External` is removed from the public enum + all tests updated, OR the executor actually waits on a registered external signal and the integration is covered by a test that registers, fires, and observes the signal.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:45:23.924074Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:29.076549Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:29.076246Z","close_reason":"Fixed in ef9c7895","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-8qd0l","title":"sync_fts_for_pane skips seq=0 segment on fresh pane (off-by-one in WHERE seq > last_indexed_seq)","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:15884 sync_fts_for_pane`:

```rust
let progress = get_fts_pane_progress_sync(conn, pane_id)?;
let last_seq = progress.as_ref().map_or(0, |p| p.last_indexed_seq);
```

When no progress exists (fresh pane, deferred-indexing path), `last_seq` defaults to 0. Then `get_unindexed_segments_sync` queries with `WHERE seq > ?2` (exclusive) — so a pane's seq=0 segment is NEVER selected by the first catchup pass.

`append_segment_sync` (storage.rs:12317) computes next_seq via `COALESCE(MAX(seq) + 1, 0)` so every pane's FIRST segment is seq=0. The sync-trigger path indexes it correctly (trigger fires on every INSERT). The deferred-catchup path does not.

# Discovery

Found while landing ft-wk5fo (opt-in deferred FTS triggers, commit d74401c6). The initial ft-wk5fo regression tests used seq 0..500 and failed with `99 expected 100` because the first round missed seq=0. Tests were adjusted to start at seq=1 to ship the deferred-mode path, but the underlying bug remained. This bead tracks fixing the bug proper.

# Fix options

(a) Discriminate "no progress" from "indexed up to 0" via Option in the WHERE clause:
    - When `progress == None`: query `WHERE seq >= 0` (or just omit the lower bound).
    - When `progress == Some(p)`: query `WHERE seq > p.last_indexed_seq`.
    Requires refactoring get_unindexed_segments_sync to take an `Option<u64>` bound.

(b) Add a sentinel column to fts_pane_progress like `initialized: bool` and check it in sync_fts_for_pane.

(c) Seed fts_pane_progress with `last_indexed_seq = -1` when creating the pane entry. Requires i64 column or underflow trick.

(a) is cleanest.

# Regression test

Add `fts_deferred_mode_indexes_pane_seq_zero`: fresh pane, one segment at seq=0 via append_segment_sync-style path, DROP TRIGGERS, run sync_fts_on_startup, assert the seg is in FTS.

# Severity

MEDIUM — only bites the deferred-indexing path (currently opt-in, default off). Trigger path is unaffected. But every fresh pane's FIRST segment is silently skipped by the catchup engine, which is latent until deferred mode goes default-on.

# Related

- ft-wk5fo (parent): introduced the deferred path that exposes this bug.  {"id":"ft-9xbxe","title":"mcp_tools test helper hits asupersync vs fastmcp_rust::asupersync version conflict","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4812\` cannot compile under
\`--features mcp\`:

\`\`\`
error[E0308]: mismatched types
    --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4812:25
     |
4812 |         McpContext::new(asupersync::Cx::for_testing(), 1)
     |         --------------- ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
     |                         expected \`fastmcp_rust::Cx\`,
     |                         found \`asupersync::Cx\`
     |
note: there are multiple different versions of crate \`asupersync\` in
      the dependency graph
\`\`\`

\`McpContext::new\` (defined in \`fastmcp_rust\`) expects
\`fastmcp_rust::Cx\` (i.e. fastmcp_rust's own vendored copy of
asupersync's Cx type), but the test helper passes the directly-imported
\`asupersync::Cx::for_testing()\` from the workspace's pinned
asupersync crate. With version drift between fastmcp_rust's vendored
asupersync (0.2.6 per the older fastmcp_rust pin) and the workspace
asupersync (0.2.9 per /Users/jemanuel/.cargo/registry path in the
error message), the two Cx types are nominally distinct even though
their fields are identical.

## Concrete failure path

1. Run \`cargo test --no-run --lib -p frankenterm-core --features mcp\`.
2. Compiler tries to build the \`#[cfg(feature = "mcp")]\` test module
   in mcp_tools.rs.
3. \`test_mcp_context()\` at line 4811-4813 fails to type-check because
   \`McpContext::new\` from fastmcp_rust expects \`fastmcp_rust::Cx\` but
   the call site passes \`asupersync::Cx\` from the workspace's
   asupersync.
4. **Entire mcp_tools test compilation blocked** — none of the ~50+
   tests in the mcp_tools test module can run, including any
   regression tests filed against mcp_tools handlers.

## Reproduction

\`\`\`
CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang \\
CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ \\
CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-rv-p0-target \\
cargo test --no-run --lib -p frankenterm-core \\
  --no-default-features --features mcp 2>&1 | grep -B2 -A10 E0308
\`\`\`

## Severity rationale

- Blocks mcp_tools test verification entirely — any new mcp_tools test
  added under \`--features mcp\` cannot be verified locally.
- Real defect-finding work in the mcp surface (e.g. ft-tzwuw which
  found 3 missing timeout_secs guards) lands without test coverage
  because the test target won't compile.
- Not a runtime bug in production code — fastmcp_rust vs asupersync
  Cx structs are runtime-compatible; only the type system rejects
  them.

## Fix sketch

One of:
1. Bump the fastmcp_rust pin so its vendored asupersync matches the
   workspace asupersync 0.2.9 (preferred — single source of truth).
2. Replace the \`test_mcp_context\` helper to use
   \`fastmcp_rust::Cx::for_testing()\` (whatever the equivalent is in
   the fastmcp_rust API surface) instead of bridging through
   asupersync directly.
3. Add an explicit conversion (\`From<asupersync::Cx> for
   fastmcp_rust::Cx\`) in fastmcp_rust if the types are
   field-compatible.

(1) is the cleanest because it eliminates the duplicated dependency.
(2) is the smallest local change.

## Scope

Diagnostic only — fix likely lives in fastmcp_rust pin (Cargo.toml)
or the test helper. Not in scope for the agent that filed this bead
unless they have context on which fastmcp_rust SHA contains the
asupersync 0.2.9 alignment.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:52:59.938463Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:26.989722Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:26.989533Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-a0up5","title":"ingest::trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes returns more than max_bytes when last char straddles the cap","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/ingest.rs:1405\` — \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes(text, max_bytes)\`
silently returns a string LARGER than \`max_bytes\` when the last
character would not fit. The function name and downstream callers
expect a hard \`max_bytes\` cap.

## Concrete failure path

Trace \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("中", 1)\` (中 is 3 UTF-8 bytes):

1. text.len()=3, max_bytes=1. \`text.len() <= max_bytes\` (3 <= 1) is
   false; max_bytes != 0; continue.
2. start = 3.saturating_sub(1) = 2. Position 2 is in the middle of 中.
3. Snap-forward loop: is_char_boundary(2)=false → start=3.
   is_char_boundary(3)=true (text.len() is always a boundary). But the
   loop guard is \`start < text.len()\`, which is false at start=3.
   Exit with start=3.
4. \`start >= text.len()\` (3 >= 3) && max_bytes > 0 → enter the
   "fall back to last full character" branch (line 1419-1430).
5. The fallback walks backward from text.len()=3 to find the start of
   the last character (position 0 for 中), then \`saturating_sub(1)\`
   off-by-ones to position 0. (The off-by-one here is a separate issue
   from the contract violation but produces the same bytes.)
6. Returns \`text[0..]\` = "中" — **3 bytes when max_bytes was 1**.

Symmetric case: \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("a中", 2)\` returns "中"
(3 bytes when 2 was requested).

## Caller impact

\`enforce_segment_size_for_persistence\` (line 1434) wraps this and
captures \`kept_bytes = truncated_content.len()\` in
\`SegmentSizeEnforcement\`. If \`kept_bytes > max_bytes\`, the
\`SegmentSizeEnforcement.kept_bytes\` field reports the violation
correctly — but the actual segment is still oversized, and:

* The truncation gap reason format string says \`"max_bytes={max}"\` —
  misleading because the kept bytes exceed it.
* Downstream consumers that allocate \`max_segment_bytes\`-sized
  buffers, set FTS5 column truncation, or enforce DB-level CHECK
  constraints on segment length get unexpectedly large input.
* Any future caller that does \`assert!(result.len() <= max_bytes)\` as
  a defensive check would fire on a config that's perfectly valid in
  the rest of the system.

## Severity rationale

- Function-contract violation: name says "to_max_bytes", behavior
  doesn't enforce that.
- Reachable when an operator configures \`max_segment_bytes\` smaller
  than the largest UTF-8 char (4 bytes) AND the segment starts with
  multi-byte content. Realistic only for misconfiguration or hostile
  input designed to exercise edge cases — but the bug exists.
- The inline comment at line 1419-1422 justifies "return the last full
  character rather than returning an empty string" — that's a
  *behavior choice*, but it violates the function's declared contract.
  Either the contract should change (function name + docs) or the
  behavior should respect the cap.

## Fix sketch

Two reasonable directions:

(A) **Strict contract** (preferred — matches function name):
  In the fallback branch, if the last character's byte length exceeds
  max_bytes, return \`String::new()\` rather than returning the
  oversize char. Callers that want "best-effort" can opt in via a
  separate API.

(B) **Document the exception**:
  Rename to \`trim_utf8_tail_with_min_one_char\` or update the doc to
  state that the result MAY exceed max_bytes when max_bytes <
  smallest character. Also update \`enforce_segment_size_for_persistence\`
  to assert / handle the over-cap case explicitly.

## Regression tests

Three tests pin the contract:
- \`trim_utf8_tail_returns_at_most_max_bytes_for_multibyte_char\` —
  \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("中", 1)\` returns a string ≤ 1 byte.
- \`trim_utf8_tail_handles_2_byte_cap_with_3_byte_char\` — same for
  \`("a中", 2)\`.
- \`enforce_segment_size_respects_kept_bytes_le_max_bytes\` — the wrapper
  never produces a segment whose content exceeds max_segment_bytes.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/ingest.rs\` only. Existing tests at
lines 6051-6068 don't cover this case (max_bytes=4 with "café" tests
only the well-fitting path).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:00:25.710169Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:57.324478Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:57.324241Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4668d1bf: trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes now respects max_bytes as a hard ceiling. Removed the 'fall back to last full character' branch that returned 3-byte 中 when max_bytes=1 or 2. New behavior: when no suffix fits within max_bytes, return empty — downstream enforce_segment_size_for_persistence already wraps the oversize signal in a Gap segment with a truncation_reason, so an empty-content gap is the sound default. 3 regression tests pin: (1) trim('中', 1) and trim('中', 2) return ≤max_bytes; (2) trim('a中', 2) returns ≤2 bytes; (3) enforce_segment_size_for_persistence on '中' with max_segment_bytes=1 produces kept_bytes ≤ 1. All 164 ingest tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target including the original 3 trim_utf8_tail tests (no regression on the in-bounds path).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment.  {"id":"ft-g3z31","title":"[review][LOW] ft-ojgmo lacks regression test for pre-cancel fast-path in EventSubscriber::recv_cx","description":"fa1fbffe added two cancel-detection paths to EventSubscriber::recv_cx at crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:789-817:

1. Pre-cancel fast-path at :793:
       if cx.is_cancel_requested() { return Err(RecvError::Cancelled); }
2. Mid-flight cancel watcher at :798-806 (select race with 50ms polling).

The new regression test `event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_mid_flight_cancel` at :1573-1601 exercises the MID-FLIGHT path only — cancel is signalled 100ms AFTER recv_cx is entered. The PRE-CANCEL fast-path (cx already cancelled when recv_cx is called) is untested at the EventSubscriber layer.

Evidence: search for calls to EventSubscriber::recv_cx with a pre-cancelled cx in the test suite — none exist. The runtime_compat.rs test at :6189 tests pre-cancel on the lower-level broadcast_recv_with_cx primitive, but not the EventSubscriber wrapper that is the public API.

Why it matters: the pre-cancel shortcut is one line, but it was the difference between a hot loop of polling wakeups and immediate return. A refactor that moves the cancel check inside the select! block (losing the shortcut) would regress the behavior silently — the mid-flight test would still pass because the 50ms poll would eventually fire.

Fix: add a sibling test:

    #[test]
    fn event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_pre_cancel_immediately() {
        run_async_test(async {
            let cx = crate::cx::Cx::for_testing();
            cx.cancel_with(crate::outcome::CancelKind::User, Some("pre-cancel"));
            let bus = EventBus::new(8);
            let mut sub = bus.subscribe();
            let started = Instant::now();
            let result = sub.recv_cx(&cx).await;
            assert!(matches!(result, Err(RecvError::Cancelled)));
            assert!(started.elapsed() < Duration::from_millis(5),
                "pre-cancel must return immediately, took {:?}", started.elapsed());
        });
    }

A <5ms assertion distinguishes the shortcut path from the 50ms poll path.

Severity: LOW — not a correctness bug, just a coverage gap. Discovered during reviewer audit round 11, 2026-04-23 while verifying fa1fbffe.

 {"id":"ft-g82hb","title":"[reality-gap] README config example advertises keys that parse but are silently ignored","description":"The README configuration example under `## Configuration` does not match the effective config surface of the shipped binary.

Evidence:
- A reconstructed snippet containing `workflows.enabled = true` fails `ft config validate` with `invalid type: boolean true, expected a sequence`.
- When rewritten as `enabled = []` so the file parses, `ft config export --path ... --json` drops or ignores several documented keys:
  - `workflows.concurrency` is ignored; effective config still uses `max_concurrent`.
  - `safety.approve_new_hosts` is ignored.
  - `safety.redact_secrets` is ignored.
  - `patterns.packs = ["core"]` parses, but the effective config surface uses builtin-qualified pack names.

This is dangerous because operators can paste the README block, get either a hard type error or a config that validates while silently not applying the documented knobs.

Expected fix:

+# 2026-04-23 Swarm session — RusticMeadow summary
+
+One agent's slice of a multi-pane session. 116 commits across all panes;
+**59 commits** carry RusticMeadow co-authorship. Convergence declared after
+5 consecutive reviewer rounds landed no substantive defects (ft-fy44g).
+
+## Top P0/P1 finds + fixes landed
+
+1. **ft-ljgyr [P1 compile blocker]** — `WaSendTool::with_wezterm_handle_and_shared_rate_limiter`
+   was `#[cfg(test)]` but called from non-test constructors (a slip in pane 2's
+   ft-eu0no `SharedRateLimiter` refactor). E0599 was blocking every non-test
+   build; un-gated the constructor in `810ad4c9`. Swarm unblocked.
+
+2. **ft-x86z2 [P1 security]** — 8 mutating MCP tools (`wa.tx_run`/`rollback`,
+   `wa.mission_abort`/`pause`/`resume`, `wa.events_annotate`/`triage`/`label`)
+   were skipping `PolicyEngine::authorize` while their peer mutators gated.
+   Shipped a shared `mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation` gate in `a4b7c8c3` covering
+   5 of 8 (tx + mission); the 3 events tools needed a constructor-signature
+   refactor and got filed as ft-vue5t.
+
+3. **ft-yj375 [P1 redaction leak]** — `wa.state` MCP tool + `ft robot state`
+   CLI both returned pane `title` / `cwd` **unredacted**, while the peer web
+   `/panes` handler did redact them. Pane titles can carry agent-supplied
+   credentials (e.g. `codex --api-key sk-...`). Fixed at the serving handler
+   (commit from pane 3); matrix at `docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md`
+   flipped to ✓.
+
+4. **ft-h90rh → ft-rsqap → ft-mw1zb [security/persistence]** — built a
+   three-layer staircase so every policy-denied MCP mutation now persists to
+   a dedicated SQLite audit stream. Storage infra + schema v24 migration +
+   `PolicyDeniedAuditRecord` in `4595cc0e`, 5-deny-paths wiring via gate
+   helper in `c3d273f0`, 6-more direct sites wired in `97016fe6`. 11 of 12
+   deny paths now persist (wa.send deliberately skipped because
+   `PolicyGatedInjector` already audits to `audit_actions`).
+
+5. **ft-kbbc3 [P1 tx integrity] (filed, not fixed)** — `wa.tx_run` builds
+   synthetic prepare-gate inputs (`tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all`) and
+   always-success commit/compensation inputs, then **persists the fabricated
+   `Committed` state to the tx contract file on disk**. `PaneStepExecutor` /
+   `TxExecutionEngine` exist but aren't wired to the MCP handler. Substantial
+   refactor; filed with two interim-mitigation options.

+  taking).
